Alaska Department of Health Releases Provisional Suspected-Overdose Surveillance Update
Provisional data from the Alaska syndromic surveillance system indicate a decrease of approximately 3 percent in suspected nonfatal overdose emergency department encounters compared with the same period one year earlier. Surveillance counts are preliminary and subject to revision as additional records are reported and toxicology completes. The Department continues to publish these data monthly through its DOSE-SYS dashboard alongside national CDC data. --- Alaska Department of Health Notes Increasing Co-Involvement of Methamphetamine and Fentanyl
Toxicology data from Alaska continue to show increasing co-involvement of methamphetamine and fentanyl in fatal and nonfatal overdose events; approximately 40 percent of fentanyl-positive overdose specimens were also positive for methamphetamine in the most recent quarter. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications. --- Alaska Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Alaska medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 52 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Alabama Department of Public Health Announces Recovery Housing Capacity Expansion
A $9,000,000 State Opioid Response grant will fund approximately 416 additional recovery housing beds across 19 provider organizations in Alabama. Recovery housing offers a substance-free living environment for individuals in recovery from opioid and other substance use disorders and is associated with improved treatment retention and reduced overdose recurrence. Funded providers must meet National Alliance for Recovery Residences standards.

Arkansas Department of Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Arkansas that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 59 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present.

Arizona Department of Health Services Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
Arizona health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 9 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Mesa area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for benzodiazepine analogues. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the Arizona Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help. --- Arizona Department of Health Services Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Arizona that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 71 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present. --- Arizona Department of Health Services Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 194 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the Arizona treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

California Department of Public Health Opens Naloxone Vending Machines and Harm-Reduction Kiosks
Naloxone vending machines providing free Narcan nasal spray, fentanyl test strips, and overdose education materials are now operational at 5 locations across California, including Sacramento, Los Angeles, San Diego. The machines operate 24 hours a day and require no identification, prescription, or fee. Use of the machines is anonymous and protected under state public health authority. The Department will continue to expand kiosk placement in partnership with libraries, transit hubs, and community organizations. --- California Department of Public Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl

DC Health Authorizes Expanded Syringe Services Program Coverage
The Department has approved expansion of authorized syringe services programs to additional counties under the District of Columbia public health authority. Syringe services programs reduce transmission of HIV and hepatitis C, connect individuals to medication for opioid use disorder, and distribute naloxone and fentanyl test strips. Decades of peer-reviewed evidence and CDC guidance support syringe services programs as a core component of the public health response to drug-related harms. --- DC Health Announces Recovery Housing Capacity Expansion
A $8,000,000 State Opioid Response grant will fund approximately 138 additional recovery housing beds across 19 provider organizations in the District of Columbia. Recovery housing offers a substance-free living environment for individuals in recovery from opioid and other substance use disorders and is associated with improved treatment retention and reduced overdose recurrence. Funded providers must meet National Alliance for Recovery Residences standards.

Delaware Division of Public Health Launches Quick Response Teams in Additional Counties
Quick Response Teams (QRTs) consisting of behavioral health peers, paramedics, and law enforcement liaisons will begin operations in Wayne, Multnomah, Jefferson counties. QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $2,300,000 HRSA grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division.

Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services Launches Quick Response Teams in Additional Counties

Beginning this month the Department will support a pilot program providing fentanyl test strips and xylazine immunoassay strips through approximately 23 authorized harm reduction sites across Kentucky. Drug checking allows individuals who use drugs to detect adulterants prior to use, an evidence-based intervention shown to reduce overdose risk. Test strips are legal under Kentucky statute when used for harm reduction and are distributed at no cost to the recipient. Site locations are listed on the Department's harm reduction webpage. --- Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections

The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

Louisiana Department of Health Announces Expanded Over-the-Counter Naloxone Availability
Following the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval of over-the-counter naloxone nasal spray, Louisiana Department of Health confirms that Narcan is now available without a prescription at participating retail pharmacies statewide, including major chains. The Department continues to operate 137 community distribution sites and supplies free naloxone to approximately 60 community-based organizations through state and federal opioid response funding. Residents may also request free naloxone by mail through the state harm-reduction program. The Department reminds the public that naloxone reverses opioid overdose only and is safe to administer; bystanders cannot harm a person who does not have opioids in their system.

Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present. --- Alabama Department of Public Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Alabama medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 35 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

Vermont Department of Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl
Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Vermont that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 71 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present. --- Vermont Department of Health Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
Vermont health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 6 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Rutland area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for methamphetamine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the Vermont Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help.

Wisconsin Department of Health Services Announces Recovery Housing Capacity Expansion
A $10,000,000 State Opioid Response grant will fund approximately 152 additional recovery housing beds across 20 provider organizations in Wisconsin. Recovery housing offers a substance-free living environment for individuals in recovery from opioid and other substance use disorders and is associated with improved treatment retention and reduced overdose recurrence. Funded providers must meet National Alliance for Recovery Residences standards.

West Virginia Department of Health and Human Resources Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 74 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the West Virginia treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Stimulant-opioid co-use poses unique clinical challenges; naloxone reverses the opioid component but stimulant toxicity may require separate medical management including supportive care and monitoring for cardiovascular complications.

Oklahoma State Department of Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
The Department announces the addition of approximately 82 new buprenorphine-prescribing clinicians to the Oklahoma treatment network as a result of the federal removal of the X-waiver requirement and continued state-led training programs. Buprenorphine, methadone, and extended-release naltrexone remain the first-line treatments for opioid use disorder. Residents seeking medication for opioid use disorder may use the SAMHSA treatment locator at FindTreatment.gov or contact the state addiction services helpline. Medicaid covers all three medications and counseling services without prior authorization.

Pennsylvania Department of Health Authorizes Expanded Syringe Services Program Coverage
The Department has approved expansion of authorized syringe services programs to additional counties under Pennsylvania public health authority. Syringe services programs reduce transmission of HIV and hepatitis C, connect individuals to medication for opioid use disorder, and distribute naloxone and fentanyl test strips. Decades of peer-reviewed evidence and CDC guidance support syringe services programs as a core component of the public health response to drug-related harms.

Rhode Island Department of Health Issues Public Health Advisory on Cluster of Suspected Fentanyl-Involved Overdoses
Rhode island health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 16 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Providence area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for xylazine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the Rhode Island Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help. --- Rhode Island Department of Health Launches Quick Response Teams in Additional Counties
Quick Response Teams (QRTs) consisting of behavioral health peers, paramedics, and law enforcement liaisons will begin operations in Marion, Allegheny, Kanawha, Davidson counties. QRTs follow up with overdose survivors within 72 hours of an emergency response, offering naloxone, fentanyl test strips where authorized, and warm handoffs to medication for opioid use disorder treatment. Funding for the expansion comes from a $1,900,000 Bureau of Justice Assistance grant administered by the Department's behavioral health division.

South Carolina Department of Public Health Reminds Residents of 911 Good Samaritan Overdose Protections
Under South Carolina law, individuals who call 911 to report an overdose are protected from prosecution for possession of a controlled substance for personal use. The Department urges anyone witnessing an overdose to call 911 immediately, administer naloxone if available, place the person in the recovery position, and remain with them until emergency responders arrive. Bystander intervention saves lives; hesitation due to fear of arrest is the leading reason calls are delayed.

Tablets sold as oxycodone, hydrocodone, Adderall, and Xanax continue to be seized in Alabama that, on laboratory analysis, contain illicitly manufactured fentanyl. The Drug Enforcement Administration has reported that approximately 67 percent of counterfeit pills tested nationally contain a potentially lethal dose of fentanyl. Residents are urged to obtain medications only from licensed pharmacies and to assume that any pill obtained from a non-pharmacy source may contain fentanyl regardless of its appearance. Naloxone should be available wherever opioid pills, illicit or otherwise, are present.

California Department of Public Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from California medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 42 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone. --- California Department of Public Health Expands Access to Medication for Opioid Use Disorder

Washington State Department of Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply

Toxicology reports from Delaware medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 33 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Hawaii Department of Health Issues Alert on Detection of Nitazene Synthetic Opioids
State public health laboratory results have detected isotonitazene, N-pyrrolidino-protonitazene, and related synthetic opioids in samples submitted from 2 jurisdictions in the past 30 days. Nitazene analogues are extremely potent — some exceeding fentanyl in laboratory potency assays — and may not be reliably detected by point-of-care fentanyl test strips alone. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; multiple sequential doses may be required. Clinicians treating suspected opioid overdose should consider repeat naloxone administration, airway management, and prolonged observation. --- Hawaii Department of Health Warns Public of Counterfeit Pills Containing Fentanyl

Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from Nebraska medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 44 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. DC Health Warns of Xylazine Mixed Into Illicit Drug Supply
Toxicology reports from the District of Columbia medical examiners and law-enforcement seizures continue to show xylazine, a non-opioid veterinary sedative, mixed into the illicit fentanyl supply. Xylazine — also known as 'tranq' — was detected in approximately 50 percent of fentanyl-positive specimens analyzed in the past quarter. Xylazine causes prolonged sedation and can produce severe soft-tissue wounds. Naloxone reverses the opioid component of these mixtures and should still be administered when overdose is suspected. Individuals presenting with non-healing wounds should seek medical evaluation and be screened for substance use treatment options including buprenorphine and methadone.

Wyoming health officials are alerting clinicians, first responders, and the public to a recent cluster of 7 suspected nonfatal overdose events reported in the Cheyenne area. Preliminary toxicology indicates involvement of illicitly manufactured fentanyl, with several specimens also positive for methamphetamine. Naloxone remains the recommended reversal agent; clinicians should be prepared to administer repeat doses given fentanyl potency. Community members are encouraged to obtain naloxone through participating pharmacies or county health departments at no cost. Anyone witnessing an overdose should call 911; the Wyoming Good Samaritan law provides limited protection from prosecution for those seeking help.
